picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation

The momentum screen flagged 4/4 EPS beats with record data-center revenue, and the sector scorecard confirms semis as the #1 or #2 sector on all RS timeframes. Cantor's $350 target implies 70%+ upside; the Blackwell architecture ramp and sovereign AI deals provide multi-year revenue visibility that dwarfs any near-term macro headwind.

picks.catalystWindow: August 2026 earnings + next 4 weeks on geopolitical resolution
picks.entryNote: Trading at $205, ~13% off the $236 52-week high; P/E of 31.8x is the lowest trailing multiple since early 2023 given the EPS explosion. Today's -1% FOMC selloff provides a marginal improvement on an already constructive entry; consolidating above the 50dma support zone.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.

picks.catalystWindow: Late July earnings + next 2-4 weeks on macro relief

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.

AWS AI margin expansion is the core thesis: as AI inference workloads scale on Amazon Bedrock and Trainium 2 chips, AWS operating margins expand structurally, with the advertising flywheel and logistics network providing defensive earnings diversification. AMZN's 52-week high of $278 vs. current ~$258 level (FOMC sell-off today per -3.46%) creates a re-entry window into a multi-year compounder.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — AWS re-acceleration guidance and operating margin trajectory; Amazon quantum computing timeline announcement already moving the stock this week
picks.catalystWindow: Late July 2026 earnings season
picks.entryNote: Trading below the $278 52-week high after a -3.46% FOMC session; YTD return of 6.58% leaves it well below SPY's 11% YTD — a relative underperformance that creates asymmetric catch-up potential given the earnings trajectory.

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.

Today's FOMC selloff provides a tactical entry improvement on what remains a fundamentally clean momentum name.

picks.catalystWindow: Next 2 quarters; Llama 4 commercial deployment updates in July-August
picks.entryNote: Consensus targets cluster $811-$897 vs. current price ~$620; forward P/E remains reasonable relative to the earnings beat cadence. Today's selloff of broader tech provides a marginal entry improvement against an already-well-owned name.
picks.exitTrigger: Digital ad market share loss to TikTok exceeding 300bps; regulatory forced divestiture of Instagram or WhatsApp; Llama-driven AI cost escalation that compresses FCF margins below 30%
picks.keyRisk: Ad spending cyclicality in a stagflationary environment — if consumer confidence deteriorates sharply, brand advertisers cut budgets and Meta's direct-response revenue follows with a 1-2 quarter lag

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Energy sector EPS estimates are up 57% for 2026 — the single largest positive revision in the entire S&P 500 — and XOM is the highest-quality, lowest-risk expression of this thesis with integrated operations, Pioneer acquisition synergies, and $15B+ annual buyback capacity. The Iran conflict has been the largest geopolitical oil supply disruption in history per Dallas Fed research, yet today's emerging U.S.-Iran peace deal signal creates an asymmetric optionality: either oil stays elevated (XOM earns more) or the peace deal materializes (CPI falls, Fed holds, multiple re-rates for all equities including XOM). Berkshire's trimming of CVX vs. holding XOM positions makes XOM the institutional-preferred major.
picks.catalyst: Potential U.S.-Iran peace deal and Strait of Hormuz reopening — paradoxically, this reduces headline CPI which unlocks Fed optionality and supports equity multiples broadly while XOM's Pioneer synergy FCF continues regardless
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4-8 weeks on geopolitical resolution; Q2 earnings late July
picks.entryNote: Energy is the #1 or #2 sector on all RS timeframes; XOM specifically has reset modestly from highs on the emerging ceasefire narrative, creating a better risk/reward entry. Forward P/E near historical median — fair value with a potential earnings upgrade cycle still underway.
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude sustained below $70/barrel for 60+ days; Pioneer integration delivering significantly less synergy FCF than guided ($2B+); punitive windfall profit tax legislation passing Congress
picks.keyRisk: U.S.-Iran peace deal fully materializing and reopening Hormuz causes oil to drop sharply — though this would be partially offset by the broad equity multiple re-rating from lower CPI

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier large-cap defense + aerospace compounder: the Iran war has structurally accelerated global defense spending timelines, and RTX's Pratt & Whitney engines plus Raytheon missiles/interceptors are directly embedded in active conflict procurement. The sector scorecard confirms defense and aerospace as multi-year budget cycle beneficiaries, and the industrials/defense sub-sector has seen consistent upward earnings revisions. RTX's backlog of $220B+ provides multi-year revenue visibility that makes it unusually resilient to a hawkish Fed — unlike rate-sensitive growth, RTX's cash flows are government-contract-indexed and inflation-linked.
picks.catalyst: Next NATO defense spending summit committing allies to 3.5% GDP defense budgets; Q2 2026 earnings with Pratt & Whitney GTF engine remediation update and missile/interceptor order backlog expansion from the Iran conflict
picks.catalystWindow: July 2026 earnings + ongoing NATO budget cycle
picks.entryNote: Defense stocks have been quietly bid throughout the Iran conflict; RTX has outperformed on 3M and 6M RS windows per the sector scorecard. Trading at a reasonable 20-22x forward earnings for a business with $220B backlog and structurally rising global defense budgets.
picks.exitTrigger: Iran peace deal fully resolved AND NATO defense spending commitments reversed; Pratt & Whitney GTF geared turbofan engine failure rate exceeding litigation threshold; margin compression from supply chain constraints below 12% operating margin
picks.keyRisk: U.S.-Iran ceasefire reduces near-term urgency of defense procurement cycles, causing a multiple de-rating from current elevated defense valuations

picks.name: The Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: The financial sector defied the broader market trend with a 1.2% upward EPS revision while S&P 500 estimates fell ~3% — and GS is the purest play on the capital markets recovery thesis. M&A volumes are recovering from multi-year lows, Goldman's trading business benefits from elevated volatility (today's FOMC-driven market swings are net positive for FICC revenues), and the dot plot's hawkish lean means rates stay higher-for-longer which is net positive for net interest income and trading spreads. The scorecards flag financials as transitioning from defensive value to 'primary driver of market momentum.'
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — investment banking fees, FICC trading revenues, and asset management AUM growth against the backdrop of today's elevated volatility environment
picks.catalystWindow: Mid-July 2026 earnings; ongoing M&A pipeline re-opening through 2H 2026
picks.entryNote: XLF at $53.56 with continued momentum per the sector scorecard; financials have seen forward P/E ratios compress even as profitability improves — creating a cheap-to-fair entry. Higher-for-longer rates from today's hawkish dot plot are a direct catalyst for GS trading revenues.
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenue declining more than 20% QoQ indicating M&A pipeline seizure; credit losses spiking from leveraged loan book in a recession; regulatory capital surcharge increases materially above Basel III endgame estimates
picks.keyRisk: Recession scenario — if the hawkish Fed tips growth sharply negative, M&A volumes collapse and GS's investment banking revenues revert to 2022-level lows

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality compounder in Financials — a duopoly ratings business (with Moody's) plus Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights that generate 60%+ operating margins and near-100% FCF conversion. In an environment where HY issuance is active (HY OAS at 263bps with all-in yields attractive) and IG spreads are stable at 73bps, SPGI earns fees on every bond that is issued. The Platts/Commodity Insights segment benefits directly from elevated energy prices and geopolitical complexity that requires sophisticated commodity data. This is a toll road on capital markets activity with almost no credit risk of its own.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings showing ratings revenue recovery as corporate bond issuance accelerates in 2H 2026; Commodity Insights segment benefiting from Iran-war-driven energy data demand
picks.catalystWindow: Late July 2026 earnings
picks.entryNote: SPGI has been a consistent top-10 name in the portfolio for 7 consecutive days — thesis continuity intact. Trades at a slight premium to historical forward P/E but justified by the quality of the earnings stream and structural growth of its data/analytics businesses.
picks.exitTrigger: Bond issuance volumes collapsing in a credit freeze scenario; algorithmic ratings competition materially eroding market share; DOJ antitrust action re-opening the Platts/SPGI merger consent decree
picks.keyRisk: Debt capital markets recession — if the hawkish Fed causes credit spreads to widen sharply and bond issuance to collapse, ratings revenues would fall 20-30% as happened in 2022

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: MRK is the highest-conviction defensive-growth pick in the Healthcare sector — Keytruda (pembrolizumab) is the world's best-selling oncology drug, generating $25B+ annually, and the pipeline (MK-7264 for COPD, subcutaneous Keytruda formulation to extend patent protection post-2028) provides meaningful lifecycle management upside. The sector scorecard flags Healthcare as a mean-reversion candidate with XLV at $152 underperforming despite reasonable 19.94x forward P/E. MRK specifically benefits from the managed care overhang NOT applying to it — it's a pure pharma/biotech play without UNH-related noise.
picks.catalyst: Subcutaneous Keytruda FDA approval (extends patent runway meaningfully past 2028 cliff); Q2 2026 earnings update on Keytruda volume growth and Winrevair (pulmonary hypertension) commercial launch trajectory
picks.catalystWindow: Q2 earnings July 2026; SubQ Keytruda FDA decision H2 2026
picks.entryNote: XLV at $152 per the scorecard with noted underperformance — this is the mean-reversion entry point the scorecard flagged. MRK specifically trades at ~12x forward earnings, a discount to both its own history and the broader Healthcare sector, implying the Keytruda cliff fear is overcalibrated given the SubQ lifecycle extension.
picks.exitTrigger: FDA rejection of subcutaneous Keytruda formulation without a clear re-submission path; competing PD-1/PD-L1 combination regimen taking >15% of Keytruda's addressable market; IRA Medicare price negotiation targeting Keytruda before 2028
picks.keyRisk: Keytruda patent cliff in 2028 — if biosimilar entries accelerate and the SubQ formulation does not receive FDA approval, the revenue concentration in one molecule creates a severe EPS reset in 2028-2030

picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: PWR is the premier grid electrification play — the engineering and construction company that builds the power infrastructure for AI data centers, renewable interconnection, and grid hardening. The momentum screen and sector scorecard both confirm that AI-driven energy demand is a multi-year structural tailwind for grid infrastructure that is regulatory-agnostic and rate-insensitive (government and utility-contracted revenues). The Iran war has highlighted energy security as a national priority, accelerating permitting for domestic grid investment. PWR was added on June 15 and the thesis has only strengthened with today's higher-for-longer FOMC outcome which incentivizes utilities to build more contracted, rate-base infrastructure.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — backlog growth update reflecting AI data center grid connections and DOE grid resilience program awards; any federal infrastructure spending bill provisions
picks.catalystWindow: August 2026 earnings; ongoing data center construction buildout through 2027-2028
picks.entryNote: PWR added to the portfolio on June 15 per the prior day's rotation — no thesis break. Grid electrification is a secular, not cyclical, theme. Trading at a fair mid-cycle multiple with backlog visibility that provides 18-24 months of revenue certainty.
picks.exitTrigger: AI data center construction capex pulled back meaningfully by hyperscalers, reducing new grid interconnection projects; DOE permitting delays extending project timelines >24 months; labor cost escalation compressing field operating margins below 10%
picks.keyRisk: Permitting reform failure — if federal grid permitting reform stalls in Congress, PWR's organic backlog growth assumptions are too optimistic and the stock's multiple would compress

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the highest-conviction defensive position in a stagflationary regime where the real Fed funds rate is barely positive (~4.25% nominal minus 3.77% headline PCE), dollar credibility faces multi-year fiscal headwinds, and the geopolitical risk premium from the Iran war has not been fully unwound. Today's hawkish dot plot — with 9/18 members projecting hikes — paradoxically supports gold by validating that inflation is structural, not transient. GLD provides uncorrelated tail-risk insurance against both the AI capex cycle bust and the stagflation trap scenarios identified in the risk assessment.
picks.catalyst: Sustained real rate compression scenario if growth disappoints in H2 2026; geopolitical escalation in the Middle East if the Iran peace deal stalls; any fiscal shock (debt ceiling, sovereign credit concern) driving dollar weakness
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — gold is a continuous hold, not event-driven
picks.entryNote: GLD has been the #1 defensive holding in the portfolio for all 7 prior trading days. The real rate environment remains supportive and today's hawkish FOMC outcome did not materially change the gold thesis. No overextension flags.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed pivots decisively toward rate cuts AND real rates move meaningfully positive (above +150bps real) on falling inflation; Iran peace deal fully resolves the geopolitical premium AND dollar strengthens past DXY 108
picks.keyRisk: A rapid, clean disinflation scenario — if June CPI prints well below expectations (e.g., 3.0% YoY) and the Hormuz reopens simultaneously, gold faces a sharp multi-percent correction

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides systematic diversification away from mega-cap AI concentration risk — the S&P 500 is at 212% of GDP (highest since 1929) with 30% held by the five largest names, a concentration level last seen at the dot-com peak. SCHD screens for dividend growth, financial quality, and reasonable valuation across financials, healthcare, industrials, and staples, providing natural factor exposure to the late-cycle value rotation that the sector scorecard anticipates. The ~3.5% yield provides carry that cushions drawdowns in a hawkish rate environment. This is a permanent portfolio holding, not a tactical trade.
picks.catalyst: Mean reversion in equal-weight vs. mega-cap growth — the 86% vs. 43% 3-year return gap between S&P 500 cap-weight and equal-weight historically mean-reverts; any broadening of market breadth beyond the AI names benefits SCHD's diversified composition
picks.catalystWindow: 2-4 quarters; a regime change catalyst could accelerate the timeline
picks.entryNote: Held continuously for all 7 prior trading days. The equal-weight and dividend-quality factor is a structural underweight in the current tape that we're deliberately maintaining as a portfolio diversifier. No valuation concern at current prices.
picks.exitTrigger: Mega-cap AI names re-rate to reasonable multiples (fwd P/E <18x for SPY) eliminating the concentration risk premium; or recession scenario makes defensive value less attractive than pure Treasuries
picks.keyRisk: Dividend growth stocks underperform in a continued narrow mega-cap rally — SCHD may lag SPY materially if the Mag-7 continue to dominate without broadening

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B with $397.4B in cash and short-term T-bills is generating ~5% risk-free yield on its float while maintaining optionality to deploy capital opportunistically — the exact posture that wins in late-cycle stagflationary environments. Greg Abel's Q1 portfolio moves (GOOGL +204%, DAL new, payment processors exited) show disciplined capital allocation rather than passive maintenance. In a risk-off scenario triggered by today's hawkish FOMC outcome, BRK.B historically outperforms SPY by 5-15 percentage points. Trading at ~1.4x book with the insurance float advantage fully intact, this is a cash-rich fortress with embedded call options on a market dislocation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 13F filing (~August 14) revealing Greg Abel's first independent capital allocation decisions; any major equity deployment from the $397B cash hoard would be a re-rating event; Berkshire's insurance operations benefiting from hard pricing cycle
picks.catalystWindow: August 2026 13F filing; ongoing insurance premium cycle
picks.entryNote: Held continuously for all 7 prior trading days. BRK.B at ~1.4x book represents a fair entry for a business generating double-digit ROTCE with $339B in T-bills earning 4.25%+. Today's risk-off FOMC tape is exactly the environment where BRK.B's defensive characteristics shine.
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large, highly-leveraged acquisition that impairs the balance sheet flexibility; or insurance reserve inadequacy emerges from catastrophic event losses; or the $397B cash hoard is deployed into a single large bet at peak cycle valuations
picks.keyRisk: Opportunity cost in a sustained bull market — if the AI-driven rally continues without recession, BRK.B's mega-cash position drags on returns vs. a fully-invested equity portfolio

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF (7-10Y Treasuries) is the most tactically appropriate duration hedge given today's FOMC outcome — the 2Y yield jumped 16bps to 4.216% and the 10Y sits at 4.39-4.50%, creating an attractive carry yield while the curve begins to provide negative correlation to equity drawdowns. Unlike TLT (20Y+), IEF's intermediate duration limits the mark-to-market loss in a further rate-rise scenario while still providing meaningful flight-to-quality appreciation in any genuine recession or risk-off event. With J.P. Morgan projecting the next action is a 25bp hike in September 2027, the current IEF yield is close to cyclically attractive.
picks.catalyst: Any growth disappointment in H2 2026 (payrolls falling below 75K, GDP revision toward 1%) would trigger a flight-to-quality bid; a Hormuz reopening causing energy-driven CPI collapse could also re-price rate cuts back into the curve
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — next key data points are July 14 CPI release and September FOMC
picks.entryNote: Held continuously for all 7 prior trading days. Today's 2Y yield spike to 4.216% has priced considerable hawkishness — IEF now offers a higher carry entry than existed one week ago, improving the risk/reward on a symmetric basis.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed explicitly signals two or more hikes in 2026 in a follow-up communication, pushing 10Y yields above 5.0%; or fiscal concerns cause a term premium blowout where the Treasury market sells off simultaneously with equities (stagflation correlation breakdown)
picks.keyRisk: Persistent inflation forcing the Fed to hike twice in 2026 — if the dot plot materially reprices toward 4.25-4.50% year-end 2026, IEF would face -5% to -8% losses on top of the already-elevated rate environment

picks.name: Constellation Energy Corporation
picks.rationale: CEG is a unique defensive pick that combines the inflation-protection of physical energy infrastructure with the secular growth tailwind of AI data center power demand — making it the one utility that actually benefits from the same AI buildout driving the growth book. Nuclear power provides zero-carbon, always-on baseload electricity that AI hyperscalers are actively contracting for directly (Microsoft's Three Mile Island deal, Amazon's data center nuclear agreements). In a stagflationary regime where the Fed cannot cut, CEG's long-term contracted power purchase agreements with investment-grade counterparties provide bond-like cash flow visibility with equity upside from uncontracted capacity repricing.
picks.catalyst: Additional long-term nuclear Power Purchase Agreement announcements with hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, Google all actively negotiating expanded nuclear supply); federal nuclear production tax credit clarity under IRA; potential DOE loan guarantees for Crane Clean Energy Center restart
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing — nuclear PPA deal flow continues throughout 2026; next earnings August 2026
picks.entryNote: CEG was added on June 15 and held through June 16. Today's hawkish FOMC outcome is a mild headwind to utilities broadly but CEG's contracted revenue model and AI power demand thesis are relatively insensitive to the rate environment vs. regulated utilities like NEE or DUK.
picks.exitTrigger: NRC safety review resulting in extended outage at a major facility; hyperscaler data center build-out decelerating materially, reducing direct nuclear PPA demand; DOE eliminating nuclear production tax credits
picks.keyRisk: Higher-for-longer rates compress utility multiples broadly — CEG is not immune to a sector-level re-rating if 10Y yields push toward 5% and income investors rotate entirely to Treasuries

summary: Today's macro regime has materially shifted to confirmed hawkish-hold: the June 17 FOMC dot plot with 9/18 members projecting at least one 2026 rate hike, PCE revised up to 3.6%, and GDP revised down to 2.2% validates the stagflationary transition thesis — the S&P 500 closed -1.21% at 7,420 and the Nasdaq -1.34% with the 2Y yield spiking 16bps to 4.216%, confirming the rate-cut trade is dead for 2026. The growth book concentrates conviction in AI infrastructure (NVDA, GOOGL, AMZN, META) where earnings revisions remain the strongest in the market and valuation reset provides asymmetric entry, energy via XOM as the single sector with +57% EPS revision where today's emerging Iran peace deal creates a convex scenario (peace = lower CPI = broader multiple re-rating, no peace = XOM earns more), defense/industrials (RTX, PWR) as government-contracted earnings that are rate-insensitive, and Financials (GS, SPGI) benefiting from elevated volatility and higher-for-longer yield curve dynamics. Nine of 10 growth picks are carryovers with thesis continuity intact — the only subtle evolution is a medium-conviction MRK at #9 (held since June 9) firming as a mean-reversion healthcare entry. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex cycle air pocket — if hyperscalers cut 2027 capex guidance by 20%+, NVDA, AMZN, and GOOGL would all face estimate resets simultaneously; the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, CEG) is sized at exactly 20% of the book to provide meaningful ballast against this and the stagflation trap scenarios.
diversificationNote: The 10-name growth book spans 5 GICS sectors — Information Technology (NVDA), Communication Services (GOOGL, META — capped at 2 given the 3-sector limit), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX, PWR — 2 names, different end markets: defense/aerospace vs. grid electrification), Financials (GS, SPGI — 2 names: capital markets vs. data/ratings), and Health Care (MRK); GOOGL and META share the Communication Services sector label but have entirely distinct business models (cloud/search vs. social advertising) and uncorrelated risk factors. The defensive sleeve spans 4 distinct exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), conglomerate cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate-duration fixed income (IEF), and nuclear/contracted utility (CEG) — the AI-capex factor concentration in NVDA+AMZN+GOOGL is deliberately accepted given the earnings revision dominance, partially offset by XOM and RTX which are negatively correlated to a pure-AI-demand thesis.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation

The four largest hyperscalers guided collective 2026 capex of ~$725B — up 77% YoY — with most earmarked for AI infrastructure directly fueling NVDA demand. S&P Global just upgraded NVDA's credit rating citing 'insatiable demand' for AI systems. After a week of sector-wide chip selloffs, NVDA is recovering at $205 — a tactically attractive re-entry inside its 52-week range of $140.86–$236.54, with the secular thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings print (late August) with Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation and hyperscaler order book update
picks.catalystWindow: Late August 2026

RBC Capital recently upgraded Communication Services to Overweight.

picks.catalystWindow: Late July 2026

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the consensus highest-conviction name across the smartest money in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Tepper nearly doubled his stake (+98%, making it ~15% of Appaloosa), Ackman added 19% making it his #2 position, and Tiger Global lists it among its top 5. phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing for **Friday, June 13, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO DATA DASHBOARD — U.S. EQUITIES REGIME BRIEF

---

### 🔵 FED POLICY
- **Current Fed Funds Rate:** 4.25–4.50% (held since late 2025; the March 2026 dot plot projected 
the median fed funds rate falling only 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027
).
- **June 2026 Dot Plot (latest SEP):** 
In 2026, rates are now projected at 3.6% (revised up from 3.4%), and 3.4% in 2027
, reflecting a markedly more hawkish path than even March. 
PCE inflation is estimated to hit 3.0% by year-end 2025, with 2026 easing to only 2.4%.

- **Market-Implied Path (Fed Funds Futures):** 
Per CME FedWatch, futures traders now expect no rate cuts at all in 2026; earlier this year, odds favored at least one quarter-point cut, and the FOMC may even consider rate hikes.
 
Following the May CPI report, futures markets priced in the likelihood that the next move will be a *hike* in December.

- **Real Policy Rate:** With core PCE at ~3.0% and the policy rate at 4.25–4.50%, the real rate is approximately **+125–150 bps** — meaningfully above most estimates of neutral (~0.5–1.0% real), i.e., policy is **genuinely restrictive**.

---

### 🔵 YIELD CURVE
- **10-Year Treasury:** 
The yield on the 10-year Treasury note edged down to **4.53%** on Thursday (June 11).

- **30-Year Treasury:** Based on the most recent available data, 
the 30-year was at approximately **5.01%**
 in early April and has tracked higher since.
- **2-Year Treasury:** Approximately **4.25–4.35%** (tracking closely to the policy rate).
- **2s10s Spread:** Modestly positive (~+20–25 bps), having just re-inverted and re-steepened in recent weeks — a "bear steepener" driven by long-end term premium expansion, not by easing expectations.
- **3m10y:** Still near zero or slightly negative — the recession-warning spread has not fully normalized.
- **Term Premium Direction:** Rising — 
US PPI rose 6.5% YoY in May (highest since Nov 2022), with markets reinforcing expectations the Fed could raise rates this year.


---

### 🔴 INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING
- **Headline CPI (May '26):** 
The annual inflation rate rose to **4.2%** in May 2026, its highest since April 2023, up from 3.8% in April — the *third consecutive monthly acceleration* — driven by energy costs jumping 23.5% due to the Iran conflict.

- **Core CPI (May '26):** 
Core CPI climbed to **2.9% YoY**, matching forecasts and reaching its highest since September 2025.
 
Month-over-month, core rose 0.2% in May after a 0.4% surge in April.

- **PCE (Feb '26, FOMC staff):** 
Total PCE inflation was 2.8% and core PCE was 3.0% in February.

- **Supercore (Services ex-shelter):** 
Services less energy services rose 0.3% MoM after 0.5% in April
 — some deceleration but still sticky. 
Shelter rose only 0.3% MoM, half of April's gain.

- **Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING** — energy-driven at the headline; core is grinding higher on services and shelter. 
The Fed discounts energy in its deliberations, but will note that core inflation is "likely to creep upwards as the year progresses."


---

### 🔵 GROWTH — RESILIENT BUT DECELERATING
- **Atlanta Fed GDPNow (Q2 2026):** 
The GDPNow model estimated Q2 2026 real GDP growth at **4.3% SAAR** as of May 21
, down from higher early readings but still robust. 
Q1 2026 BEA advance estimate came in at 2.0% SAAR.

- **Payrolls:** 
Unemployment held at 4.4% through February; average monthly payroll change was low in Jan–Feb due to a healthcare sector strike and harsh weather, with effects expected to unwind.
 
Average hourly earnings rose 3.8% YoY through February.

- **ISM Manufacturing/Services:** Incorporated into the GDPNow update of May 21; manufacturing has been in borderline contraction/expansion territory, while services remain the primary growth engine.

---

### 🟡 CREDIT & LIQUIDITY — MIXED
- **HY OAS:** Spreads spiked sharply in March on Iran war fears (briefly above 400 bps) and have partially retraced but remain elevated at ~330–350 bps — well above the 250–280 bps tights seen in late 2025. Direction: **widening bias** on rate hike fears + geopolitical risk.
- **IG OAS:** ~115–125 bps, modestly wider vs. Q4 2025 tights.
- 
Per Morgan Stanley, "credit spreads have continued their widening trend since late January as concerns about AI disruption and private credit" weighed.

- **DXY:** 
The U.S. Dollar Index was at approximately **99.77** as of Friday morning (June 12), up from post-Iran lows.
 
The greenback drew support from expectations that rising energy prices and higher inflation reduce the likelihood of near-term rate cuts.

- **Financial Conditions:** Tightening — VIX elevated; 
the VIX was at 19.25 on June 12, with the prior close at 19.44.
 
After cresting above 30 in mid-March, VIX trended lower through April–May, but a sharp rally in early June pushed it back into the low 20s as semiconductor profit-taking and persistent inflation weighed on risk sentiment.


---

### 🟡 EARNINGS BACKDROP — SUPPORTIVE BUT DE-RATED
- **Forward S&P 500 EPS (2026):** 
Per Yardeni Research, consensus is ~$323/share for 2026 operating earnings — a **19% YoY boost** over 2025's $271.
 Wells Fargo estimates $315–$365 for 2026–2027.
- **Forward P/E:** 
The S&P 500 P/E peaked at 23x in October 2025 (95th percentile vs. 30-year history) and has since fallen to ~20x (73rd percentile).
 Trailing earnings yield (inverse of trailing P/E) is approximately 
3.14%
 — still thin versus a 4.53% 10-year yield, meaning the equity risk premium (ERP) is near zero or negative.
- **Tech Concentration Risk:** 
Ten S&P 500 stocks represent nearly 40% of the index's value — all with AI exposure — and "a majority of earnings growth for 2026 is narrowly focused in the tech sector, accounting for nearly half" of total index EPS growth.


---

### 🔴 GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE
- **Iran War / Hormuz Strait:** 
The conflict has transformed from a kinetic event to an economic siege, with the U.S. bottling up Iran's oil exports while Iran's military remnants attempt to disrupt regional shipments through Hormuz.
 
Energy prices rose 3.9% in May amid Iran war disruptions; the energy index accounted for over 60% of the overall CPI increase.

- **Peace Deal Hopes (June 12):** 
Stocks rose Friday on reports a peace deal might be near that would lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, though markets wavered amid conflicting reports; crude oil fell ~2% to near $85/bbl.

- **Fed Hike Risk:** 
President Trump has vowed additional strikes on Iran and threatened to target Kharg Island's energy infrastructure
, a further upside inflation risk that could force the Fed's hand.

---

## ⚡ THREE-PARAGRAPH BRIEF

### 1. 🟠 REGIME CALL: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**
The U.S. macro regime is firmly **late-cycle with stagflationary characteristics** — the rare and dangerous combination of re-accelerating inflation (headline CPI at 4.2%, core PCE at 3.0%, and trending higher) running concurrently with a Fed that has fully abandoned its 2026 easing bias: 
following the May CPI report, futures markets priced the next Fed move as a *hike* in December.
 Real rates are restrictive (~+125 bps above neutral), 
Q1 GDP came in at only 2.0% SAAR
 despite the GDPNow Q2 read of 4.3% (likely front-loaded demand), the yield curve is bear-steepening, the ERP is near zero, and geopolitical energy shocks are embedding into PCE. This is a regime where duration is dangerous, equities are expensive on a risk-adjusted basis, and the margin for error is minimal.

---

### 2. ✅ WHAT'S WORKING / ❌ WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** (1) **Energy / Integrated Oils** — 
energy prices up 23.5% YoY with gasoline +40.5%
 directly benefits E&P and oilfield services; (2) **Quality/AI-exposed Megacap Tech** — earnings revisions are powerfully positive; 
technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February, driven by names like Oracle and Micron.
 (3) **Defense & Aerospace** — geopolitical escalation is a structural tailwind; (4) **Short-duration / floating rate credit** — with hike risk re-priced, bank loans and CLOs hold up better than HY bonds.

**Breaking:** (1) **Rate-sensitive sectors** (Utilities, REITs, Homebuilders) — negative ERP and rising long-end yields are structurally toxic; (2) **Consumer Discretionary / Lower-income consumer** — 
high inflation is squeezing households financially, with lower-income Americans particularly impacted as they spend more of stretched paychecks on necessities.
 (3) **Broad HY credit** — spreads on a widening trajectory with rate hike risk embedded; (4) **Non-AI Tech / Speculative Growth** — 
the Nasdaq 100's forward P/E has dropped 29% from its peak
 and multiple expansion is off the table entirely.

---

### 3. 👁️ WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 2–3 CATALYSTS (Next 1–6 Months)

1. **Iran Peace Deal / Hormuz Reopening (1–4 weeks):** 
News of a potential peace deal emerged Friday, with reports it could lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
 A confirmed deal would be the single largest near-term macro relief valve: oil falls sharply, headline CPI collapses ~150 bps within 2 months, and the Fed hike narrative reverses. Watch Polymarket odds and State Dept. communications. Failure to resolve = stagflation locked in.

2. **June FOMC Decision + Updated SEP (July 28–29 FOMC):** With futures now pricing a *hike* by December and the June dot plot already hawkishly revised, 
the FOMC may even consider rate hikes this year.
 Any signal of a formal pivot to hike bias — particularly from new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh — would immediately reprice equities (ERP collapse) and trigger a vicious bear steepener in rates. 
Warsh has indicated he thinks rates can go lower on AI productivity disinflation,
 but the data trajectory is not cooperating yet.

J.P. Morgan Global Research continues to see the Fed remaining on hold for the rest of 2026, before hiking 25 bps in September 2027.
 
Warsh has recently adopted a more dovish rhetorical stance, but this is at odds with the U.S. economic outlook, where inflation is accelerating and the May jobs report indicates the labor market remains robust.
 Meanwhile, 
17 of 32 former Fed officials and staff offering projections said a rate *increase* would likely be appropriate in 2026.


**Bottom line:** No surprise data prints or shock market-moving events overnight. The macro regime from Monday's briefing stands — policy on hold at 4.25%, no cuts priced by futures markets, and upside risks to rates rather than downside. The key catalyst to watch is **tomorrow's FOMC statement and Warsh's inaugural press conference**, which will set the tone for the second half of 2026. 
The central question is whether enough policymakers pencil in a possible rate *hike* to make that the most likely outcome for 2026.

## 📊 MACRO UPDATE — Wednesday, June 17, 2026

**Material change: today's FOMC meeting is a significant development that supersedes yesterday's analysis.** The prior briefing flagged Warsh's first press conference as a scheduled event for today — it has now occurred, and the outcome is notably hawkish.

**Fed held, but SEP revised sharply hawkish.** 
The Federal Reserve left interest rates unchanged in June following Kevin Warsh's first meeting as Fed chair, with inflation rising amid higher energy prices due to the war in Iran.
 
Fed policymakers voted 12-0 to leave the benchmark federal funds rate unchanged at its current range of 3.5% to 3.75%.
 However, the new dot plot is a major hawkish surprise: 
nine of the 18 voting members now project an interest rate *hike* before the end of 2026, with six projecting two 25-basis-point hikes, while the Committee sees PCE inflation at 3.6% at year's end — up sharply from 2.7% in the March projection.

**Warsh signals a new communication regime.** 
Wednesday's FOMC statement alone signaled that the Federal Reserve is entering a new era under Chairman Warsh — the statement contained around 130 words, down from figures above 300 recorded in recent meetings.
 
Warsh explicitly said there was no forward guidance, as it was "not well suited for the current policy conjuncture."

**Bottom line for the macro regime:** The prior briefing's concern about a potential rate hike has been validated — the dot plot now *leans toward tightening*, not easing. The rate-cut trade is effectively dead for 2026. 
Markets will now look for clarity on "whether the Fed views current inflation pressures as temporary and manageable, or whether policymakers still see a need for tighter policy later in the year."
 The equity regime has shifted from "patient hold" to a more adversarial Fed backdrop, with stagflationary pressure (higher inflation, lower growth) the dominant macro risk.

### ⚡ TAIL RISK #2: STAGFLATION TRAP — FED FORCED TO HIKE INTO SLOWDOWN
**Probability: MEDIUM (20–25%)**


US CPI sits at 3.8% with the Fed on hold, cuts off the table, and markets pricing a possible December rate hike — with new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting occurring June 16–17, 2026.
 
US inflation back at 3.8% has pushed back Fed cuts, which is the single biggest reason the dollar has firmed against the bearish consensus.
 The stagflationary trap materializes if tariff-driven goods inflation re-accelerates while employment softens — forcing the Fed to choose between controlling inflation and supporting growth. 
If inflation persists and the Fed pivots away from its dovish bias — opening the door to rate hikes or tighter liquidity — that could tighten financial conditions quickly and put pressure on risk assets, especially those that benefited from easy monetary policy.


**What triggers it:** July CPI print ≥4.2%; wage growth re-accelerating on OBBBA transfer payments; oil supply shock from Middle East (US–Iran ceasefire still unsigned per current data).

**Sectors hurt most:** Long-duration growth (SNOW, PLTR, NFLX, ARM), leveraged real estate (PLD, EQIX, O), utilities (NEE, D, SO, DUK), consumer discretionary (TSLA, SBUX, TGT). **Beneficiaries:** Short-duration TBills, energy (XOM, CVX, COP), GLD.
